words.exampleNL: Hallo, hoe gaat het met je?
words.exampleEN: Hello, how are you?
words.audioHint: The 'h' is clearly pronounced. The 'a' sounds like 'ah'.

words.exampleEN: Good morning, did you sleep well?
words.audioHint: The 'g' is a guttural sound made in the back of the throat, like clearing your throat gently. The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'boot'.

words.exampleEN: Good afternoon, can I help you?
words.audioHint: Both 'g' and the final 'g' are guttural. The 'i' sounds like 'i' in 'sit'.

words.exampleEN: Good evening, welcome to the restaurant.
words.audioHint: The 'v' in Dutch is softer than in English, almost between a 'v' and an 'f'.

words.exampleEN: Good night, sleep well!
words.audioHint: The 'ch' in 'nacht' is a guttural sound, similar to the Scottish 'loch'.

words.exampleEN: Bye, see you tomorrow!
words.audioHint: The 'g' at the end is guttural. 'Dag' can mean both hello and goodbye in Dutch.

words.exampleEN: Hi! Nice to see you!
words.audioHint: The 'oi' sounds like 'oy' in 'boy'. Very casual greeting.

words.exampleEN: Hi Jan, how are you?
words.audioHint: The 'oe' in 'hoe' sounds like 'oo' in 'too'. The 'g' in 'gaat' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Goedemiddag mevrouw, hoe gaat het met u?
words.exampleEN: Good afternoon madam, how are you?
words.audioHint: 'U' is the formal 'you', pronounced like 'uu' in 'vacuum'. Use with strangers and elders.

words.exampleNL: Het gaat goed met mij, dank je.
words.exampleEN: I'm doing well, thank you.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'food'. The 'd' at the end sounds like 't'.

words.exampleEN: I'm doing great!
words.audioHint: The 'i' sounds like 'ee' in 'free'. Commonly used as a positive response.

words.exampleNL: Het gaat niet zo goed vandaag.
words.exampleEN: I'm not doing so well today.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' in 'niet' sounds like 'ee' in 'feet'.

words.exampleNL: Dank je wel voor je hulp!
words.exampleEN: Thank you for your help!
words.audioHint: The 'j' in Dutch sounds like English 'y'. The 'w' in 'wel' sounds like English 'v'.

words.exampleEN: Thank you, sir.
words.audioHint: 'U' is the formal 'you'. Use 'dank u wel' with people you don't know well.

words.exampleEN: Thanks for the food!
words.audioHint: Stress is on the second syllable. Slightly less formal than 'dank je wel'.

words.exampleNL: Mag ik een kopje koffie, alsjeblieft?
words.exampleEN: May I have a cup of coffee, please?
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like 'y', so 'je' sounds like 'yuh'. Also used when handing something to someone.

words.exampleEN: Here you go, your coffee.
words.audioHint: Formal version of 'alsjeblieft'. Use with strangers, elders, and in professional settings.

words.exampleEN: Goodbye, it was nice!
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee' in 'seen'. Literally means 'until seeing'.

words.exampleEN: Bye, see you tomorrow!
words.audioHint: Very informal and friendly. The 'oe' sounds like 'oo', followed by a short 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ga even lunchen, tot straks!
words.exampleEN: I'm going to have lunch, see you later!
words.audioHint: Used when you'll see the person again the same day.

words.exampleEN: Good evening, see you tomorrow!
words.audioHint: The 'g' in 'morgen' is guttural. Literally 'until tomorrow'.

words.exampleEN: I'm Sophie, pleased to meet you.
words.audioHint: The 'aa' is a long 'ah' sound. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Hoi, ik ben Mark. Leuk je te ontmoeten!
words.exampleEN: Hi, I'm Mark. Nice to meet you!
words.audioHint: The 'eu' in 'leuk' is a unique Dutch sound - round your lips for 'oo' but say 'uh'. The 'oe' in 'ontmoeten' sounds like 'oo'.

words.exampleEN: Hello, I am Peter.
words.audioHint: Very straightforward pronunciation. 'Ben' rhymes with English 'pen'.

words.exampleNL: Hoi, hoe heet je? Ik ben Anna.
words.exampleEN: Hi, what's your name? I'm Anna.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' in 'heet' is a long 'ay' sound. Literally means 'how are you called?'

words.exampleEN: Good afternoon, what is your name?
words.audioHint: Formal version using 'u' instead of 'je'.

words.exampleNL: Mijn naam is Sietse van de Kerkhof.
words.exampleEN: My name is Sietse van de Kerkhof.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' in 'mijn' sounds like 'eye'. This is more formal than 'ik ben'.

words.exampleEN: Sorry, I'm late.
words.audioHint: Pronounced similarly to English but with a slightly rolled 'r'.

words.exampleNL: Excuseer me, mag ik er even langs?
words.exampleEN: Excuse me, may I pass?
words.audioHint: The 'eer' sounds like 'ayr'. More formal than 'sorry'.

words.exampleEN: Pardon, could you repeat that?
words.audioHint: Borrowed from French. Stress on the second syllable. Used to get attention politely.

words.exampleNL: Geen probleem, ik help je graag.
words.exampleEN: No problem, I'm happy to help you.
words.audioHint: The 'g' in 'geen' is guttural. The 'ee' sounds like 'ay' in 'say'.

words.exampleNL: Graag gedaan, het was geen moeite.
words.exampleEN: You're welcome, it was no trouble.
words.audioHint: Both 'g' sounds are guttural. The 'aa' is a long 'ah'. Literally 'gladly done'.

words.exampleEN: Welcome to the Netherlands!
words.audioHint: The 'w' in Dutch sounds like English 'v'. Similar to English 'welcome'.

words.exampleEN: Yes, that's correct.
words.audioHint: The 'j' sounds like English 'y'. Very simple pronunciation.

words.exampleEN: No, thank you.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' sounds like 'ay' in 'day'.

words.exampleEN: Excuse me, do you speak English?
words.audioHint: The 'ee' in 'spreekt' sounds like 'ay'. Very useful phrase for beginners.

words.exampleNL: Sorry, ik spreek een beetje Nederlands.
words.exampleEN: Sorry, I speak a little Dutch.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' in 'beetje' sounds like 'ay'. 'Beetje' literally means 'a little bit'.

words.exampleNL: Sorry, ik begrijp het niet. Kunt u dat herhalen?
words.exampleEN: Sorry, I don't understand. Can you repeat that?
words.audioHint: The 'ij' in 'begrijp' sounds like 'eye'. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: I have to go, see you later!
words.audioHint: More general than 'tot straks' - doesn't imply you'll meet the same day.

words.exampleNL: Doe de groeten aan je moeder!
words.exampleEN: Give my regards to your mother!
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'boot'. 'De groeten doen' means to send regards.

words.exampleEN: Have a nice weekend!
words.audioHint: The final 'g' is guttural. Often used in farewell wishes.

words.exampleNL: Tot ziens en fijne dag nog!
words.exampleEN: Goodbye and have a nice day!
words.audioHint: The 'ij' in 'fijne' sounds like 'eye'. The final 'g' in 'dag' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: Good afternoon, Mr. Jansen.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable. The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: Dag mevrouw, kan ik u helpen?
words.exampleEN: Hello madam, can I help you?
words.audioHint: The 'v' sounds like 'f'. The 'ou' sounds like 'ow' in 'how'.

words.exampleNL: Hey Lisa, hoe is het ermee?
words.exampleEN: Hey Lisa, how's it going?
words.audioHint: Very informal. The 'ee' at the end sounds like 'ay'. Used with close friends.

words.exampleNL: Het spijt me, ik kan niet komen.
words.exampleEN: I'm sorry, I can't come.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' in 'spijt' sounds like 'eye'. Used for a sincere apology.

words.exampleEN: Don't mention it, it was nothing.
words.audioHint: The 'g' in 'geen' is guttural. The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: Je hebt morgen een examen? Succes!
words.exampleEN: You have an exam tomorrow? Good luck!
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable. Often said as a farewell wish.

words.exampleNL: Ga je naar het feest? Veel plezier!
words.exampleEN: Are you going to the party? Have fun!
words.audioHint: The 'v' sounds softer than in English. The 'ee' in 'veel' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: Bedankt voor de les, tot de volgende keer!
words.exampleEN: Thanks for the lesson, until next time!
words.audioHint: The 'g' in 'volgende' is guttural. The 'ee' in 'keer' sounds like 'ay'.
